publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 165, folios 28-31. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: My Dearest. As I remember there is a West post from here, & find there is no post from Fordhook tomorrow (Sunday). I shall leave this here this morning to be sent today.--
You give a nice account of yourself, but I am vexed at your having missed my first letter, which I suppose arrived at Exeter the very
evening of the day you left. Ford will have had the sense I imagine to forward it to Ashley. I do not make out these Western posts.

Luckily you seem to have been in a less fuss than I (silly little bad Bird!) was at not hearing from you.-- The Laundry maid has given warning, so that I must now look for a second as well as a first, & I think I shall be able to secure both, in the course of

another week.-- The cause of one's leaving, is very little to her credit. She has chosen lately to have the men constantly sitting & about in the Laundry, & Hewett found one of them dressing there. Nothing can be more improper, where there are ladies' things of all kinds constantly about, & it is allowed in no family.-- It has of course been stopped, & the Laundry maid has given warning.-- If she has
so little idea of propriety, we are better without her.--

I enclosed you a letter, which was enclosed to you for me this morning from St. James' Square. Mama supposes the volume mentioned is also there, & thinks it should certainly be returned. Will you see about this when you go to Town. How impertinent people are.--
I forward you a letter directed to the Honble. William King, somewhat after date I think.
sent an invitation to dinner for the 22nd. I have returned a polite answer, which I subjected to the Hen's inspection, declining however on account of the distance.--

Charlotte has sent me two beautiful feather hand-screens, which will be a great improvement upon the old horrid things.-- I am sorry I have not sent you the newspapers, but today I will. Mama has cut out a curious paragraph about Stonehenge,
which she asked my leave to do-- poor thing!--
I must now end. I enclose you also a note I have had from Lady Radstock.--
Your most Dear Birdie, A. A. K.
